title: Conditional Expectation
motivation: Probability theory needs a precise way to say 'the best guess of $X$ given partial information'. Elementary conditioning on events of positive probability does not survive the passage to continuous time, where every particular trajectory has probability zero. Kolmogorov's solution — conditioning on a σ-algebra — replaces pointwise formulas with a characterising property, and is the definition on which filtrations, martingales, and Markov processes are built.
definition: Let $X \in L^1(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, \mathbb{P})$ and let $\mathcal{G} \subseteq \mathcal{F}$ be a sub-σ-algebra. The conditional expectation $\mathbb{E}[X \mid \mathcal{G}]$ is the (a.s.-unique) $\mathcal{G}$-measurable, integrable random variable $Y$ satisfying $$\int_G Y\,d\mathbb{P} = \int_G X\,d\mathbb{P} \quad \text{for all } G \in \mathcal{G}.$$ Existence follows from the Radon–Nikodym theorem; for $X \in L^2$ it is the orthogonal projection of $X$ onto $L^2(\mathcal{G})$.
examples: $\mathcal{G} = \sigma(Z)$: then $\mathbb{E}[X \mid \mathcal{G}] = h(Z)$ for a measurable function $h$ — 'best guess as a function of what you observed'.
For Brownian motion and $s \le t$: $\mathbb{E}[W_t \mid \mathcal{F}_s] = W_s$ (that is the martingale property) and $\mathbb{E}[W_t^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_s] = W_s^2 + (t-s)$.
The Markov property of a diffusion says $\mathbb{E}[g(X_T) \mid \mathcal{F}_t] = \mathbb{E}[g(X_T) \mid X_t]$: the past matters only through the present.
consequences: Tower property $\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[X \mid \mathcal{G}] \mid \mathcal{H}] = \mathbb{E}[X \mid \mathcal{H}]$ for $\mathcal{H} \subseteq \mathcal{G}$; taking out what is known; conditional Jensen — the everyday algebra of stochastic analysis.
The Feynman–Kac representation is literally a conditional expectation given $X_t = x$, i.e. a function on state space defined via conditioning.
Projection interpretation makes conditional expectation the theoretical core of filtering, regression, and least-squares prediction.
